title: Troubleshoot DNS name resolution from Command Prompt
description: A Windows user can reach the network by IP address but cannot browse to websites by name. Use terminal commands to identify and correct the issue.

terminal.welcome: The user reports that websites fail by name, but direct IP connectivity appears to work. Type commands to investigate.
terminal.unknownCommandOutput: This simulation only supports the commands needed for this troubleshooting path. Try a basic Windows network diagnostic command.

commands.output:
Windows IP Configuration

Ethernet adapter Ethernet:
   Connection-specific DNS Suffix  . : lan.local
   IPv4 Address. . . . . . . . . . . : 192.168.10.84
   Subnet Mask . . . . . . . . . . . : 255.255.255.0
   Default Gateway . . . . . . . . . : 192.168.10.1
   DNS Servers . . . . . . . . . . . : 192.168.10.1
commands.summary: Collected IP configuration, gateway, and DNS server information.

evidence.title: IP Configuration
evidence.body: The workstation has a valid private IPv4 address, subnet mask, default gateway, and DNS server.

commands.output:
Pinging 8.8.8.8 with 32 bytes of data:
Reply from 8.8.8.8: bytes=32 time=18ms TTL=117
Reply from 8.8.8.8: bytes=32 time=17ms TTL=117

Ping statistics for 8.8.8.8:
    Packets: Sent = 2, Received = 2, Lost = 0 (0% loss)
commands.summary: Verified IP connectivity outside the local network.

evidence.title: IP Connectivity
evidence.body: The workstation can reach an external IP address, so the issue is not total network connectivity.

commands.output:
Server:  router.lan.local
Address:  192.168.10.1

DNS request timed out.
    timeout was 2 seconds.
*** Request to router.lan.local timed-out
commands.summary: Confirmed that DNS name resolution is failing while IP connectivity works.

evidence.title: DNS Failure
evidence.body: Name resolution fails even though external IP connectivity works.

commands.output:
Windows IP Configuration

Successfully flushed the DNS Resolver Cache.
commands.summary: Cleared the local DNS resolver cache.

commands.output:
Server:  router.lan.local
Address:  192.168.10.1

Non-authoritative answer:
Name:    example.com
Addresses:  2606:2800:220:1:248:1893:25c8:1946
          93.184.216.34
commands.summary: Verified that name resolution works after clearing the resolver cache.

evidence.title: Verification
evidence.body: DNS lookup succeeds after flushing the resolver cache.

commands.output: Successfully reset the Winsock Catalog. You must restart the computer in order to complete the reset.
commands.summary: Reset Winsock before proving it was necessary.

penalty.reason: Resetting Winsock is more disruptive than checking IP connectivity and DNS behavior first.

commands.output: Access denied. This destructive action is not appropriate for a DNS troubleshooting issue.
commands.summary: Attempted a destructive command unrelated to DNS troubleshooting.

penalty.reason: Destructive disk actions are unsafe and unrelated to a DNS name-resolution problem.

grading.summary: A strong command-line troubleshooting path checks IP configuration, verifies IP connectivity, confirms DNS failure, applies a targeted DNS cache fix, verifies name resolution, and documents the result.

title: Repair corrupted Windows system files
description: A Windows 11 user reports that built-in apps crash and Windows Update recently failed. Use command-line repair tools in the correct order and verify the repair.

terminal.welcome: You are in an elevated Command Prompt. The system is bootable but unstable. Use Windows repair commands carefully.
terminal.unknownCommandOutput: This simulation supports the Windows repair commands needed for this scenario.

commands.output:
Beginning system scan. This process will take some time.

Windows Resource Protection found corrupt files but was unable to fix some of them.
commands.summary: Ran SFC and confirmed system file corruption that could not be fully repaired yet.

evidence.title: Initial SFC Result
evidence.body: SFC found corrupt files but could not repair all of them, suggesting DISM should repair the component store first.

commands.output:
Deployment Image Servicing and Management tool

[==========================100.0%==========================]
The restore operation completed successfully.
The operation completed successfully.
commands.summary: Repaired the Windows component store with DISM.

evidence.title: DISM RestoreHealth
evidence.body: DISM successfully repaired the Windows component store.

commands.output:
Beginning system scan. This process will take some time.

Windows Resource Protection found corrupt files and successfully repaired them.
commands.summary: Ran SFC again after DISM and repaired remaining system files.

evidence.title: Second SFC Result
evidence.body: SFC successfully repaired remaining system file corruption after DISM completed.

commands.output: The system rebooted and the user confirmed built-in apps open normally.
commands.summary: Rebooted after repairs and verified the symptoms were resolved.

evidence.title: Verification
evidence.body: After reboot, the original app crash symptom no longer occurs.

commands.output:
The type of the file system is NTFS.
Cannot lock current drive. Chkdsk cannot run because the volume is in use by another process.
commands.summary: Started a disk repair path before evidence pointed to file-system damage.

penalty.reason: CHKDSK is not the best first repair for Windows component store or protected system file corruption.

commands.output: Access denied. This destructive action is not appropriate for this repair scenario.
commands.summary: Attempted a destructive disk command.

penalty.reason: Formatting the system drive is destructive and not an appropriate troubleshooting step.

grading.summary: A strong Windows repair path runs SFC to confirm corruption, uses DISM to repair the component store, reruns SFC, verifies after reboot, and documents the result.

title: Force and verify Group Policy update
description: A domain user reports that a new password policy and mapped drive policy have not applied yet. Use command-line tools to check and refresh policy.

terminal.welcome: The workstation is domain-joined and online. The user says a new policy has not applied.
terminal.unknownCommandOutput: This scenario supports the policy commands needed for this task.

commands.output:
COMPUTER SETTINGS
-----------------
    CN=CLIENT01,OU=Workstations,DC=corp,DC=local
    Last time Group Policy was applied: 2 days ago

USER SETTINGS
-------------
    The following GPOs were not applied because they were filtered out.
commands.summary: Checked current policy status and confirmed policy is stale.

evidence.title: Policy Status Before Update
evidence.body: Group Policy has not updated recently, which explains why the new settings are missing.

commands.output:
Updating policy...

Computer Policy update has completed successfully.
User Policy update has completed successfully.
commands.summary: Forced a Group Policy refresh.

evidence.title: Policy Refresh
evidence.body: Both computer and user policy updated successfully.

commands.output:
COMPUTER SETTINGS
-----------------
    Last time Group Policy was applied: today
    Applied Group Policy Objects
        Default Domain Policy
        Workstation Security Baseline

USER SETTINGS
-------------
    Applied Group Policy Objects
        Department Drive Mapping Policy
commands.summary: Verified that the expected GPOs are now applied.

evidence.title: Policy Status After Update
evidence.body: Expected workstation and user policies are now applied.

commands.output: The command is deprecated on this operating system.
commands.summary: Used an outdated policy refresh command.

penalty.reason: gpupdate /force is the modern command expected for refreshing Group Policy.

commands.output:
User name                    student
Account active               Yes
Password last set            4/11/2026
commands.summary: Checked the domain user account but did not refresh or verify Group Policy.

penalty.reason: The symptom points to stale policy application, not a disabled user account.

grading.summary: A strong Group Policy troubleshooting path checks current policy, forces an update with gpupdate /force, verifies with gpresult /r, and documents the result.

title: Identify a suspicious listening process
description: A Windows workstation is running slowly and a security tool reported an unexpected listening port. Use command-line tools to identify and stop the suspicious process.

terminal.welcome: The workstation is online but sluggish. A security alert mentions an unexpected listening port.
terminal.unknownCommandOutput: This scenario supports process and network inspection commands needed for the investigation.

commands.output:
Active Connections

  Proto  Local Address          Foreign Address        State           PID
  TCP    0.0.0.0:3389           0.0.0.0:0              LISTENING       936
  TCP    0.0.0.0:4444           0.0.0.0:0              LISTENING       4321
  TCP    192.168.10.84:49720    13.107.42.14:443       ESTABLISHED     1080
commands.summary: Found an unexpected listening port and associated PID.

evidence.title: Listening Port
evidence.body: Port 4444 is listening under PID 4321 and should be investigated.

commands.output:
Image Name                     PID Session Name        Mem Usage
========================= ======== ================ ============
unknownsvc.exe                 4321 Services             84,216 K
commands.summary: Mapped the suspicious PID to an unexpected process name.

evidence.title: Process Identification
evidence.body: PID 4321 maps to unknownsvc.exe, which is not expected on this workstation.

commands.output: SUCCESS: The process with PID 4321 has been terminated.
commands.summary: Stopped the suspicious process.

commands.output:
Active Connections

  Proto  Local Address          Foreign Address        State           PID
  TCP    0.0.0.0:3389           0.0.0.0:0              LISTENING       936
  TCP    192.168.10.84:49720    13.107.42.14:443       ESTABLISHED     1080
commands.summary: Verified the suspicious listening port is no longer present.

evidence.title: Verification
evidence.body: Port 4444 is no longer listening after stopping the suspicious process.

commands.output:
Windows IP Configuration

Ethernet adapter Ethernet:
   Connection-specific DNS Suffix  . :
commands.summary: Released the workstation's IP address without evidence of a DHCP problem.

penalty.reason: The issue is a suspicious listening process, not DHCP lease failure.

commands.output: Access denied. This destructive action is not appropriate as an initial response.
commands.summary: Attempted destructive remediation before identifying scope.

penalty.reason: Destructive disk actions are unsafe and unrelated to identifying a suspicious listening process.

grading.summary: A strong process investigation checks listening ports, maps the PID to a process, stops the suspicious process, verifies the port is gone, and documents the result.
